4. Abstract Integration

4.1. Motivation

In Riemann integration, we say the integral is well defined if

(43)

where is a partition and and are approximations of integral from below and above respectively. The and are taken with respect to (all) sequences of whose max width converges to zero. We can’t integrate things like using Riemann integration: For any interval, it contains both a rational and irrational, so the max and min are 1 and 0, hence and for all .

We would like abstract integration to satisfy these properties:

Property 9 is the monotone convergence theorem (MCT).

4.2. Simple Functions

A function is called a simple function if it is measurable and takes only finitely many different values. In particular, a simple function can be written as a finite sum of indicator functions. E.g. the indicator function over rationals in is a simple function.

If is a simple function, it’s integral is defined by

(44)

(If and , we assume , which is a measure theory convention.)

When is a probability measure, we call a simple random variable and is the expectation. A discrete RV in contrast can take on countably many values.

4.3. Nonnegative Function and General Functions

For a nonnegative (extended-valued) measurable function , let be the set of all nonnegative simple functions that satisfy , and define . Concretely we can approximate with the following sequence:

(45)

is a quantized version of , and for every is simple, pointwise. This suggests a nonnegative function is measurable if and only if it is the monotonic and pointwise limit of simple functions.

A supremum definition doesn’t work even if the function is bounded. See problem 6.5

4.7. [REVIEW] Product Measures and Fubini’s Theorem

We use product σ-algebra to describe independent variables.

Definition 14 (Product Measure). We define as the smallest -field of subsets of that contains all sets of the form and , where and .

Equivalently, can be defined as the smallest -field containing all sets of the form .

Theorem 16. There exists a unique measure on that satisfies notion of independence:

(48)

The theory here applies to probability measures and more generally measures that are -finite, meaning that the set can be partitioned into a countable union of sets, each of which has finite measure. E.g. with the Lebesgue measure on .

Fubini’s theorem applies differently to nonnegative and general functions.

Theorem 17 (Fubini).
